figures.caption: Figure 3: Distribution of hint-following behavior by model family. Left: When models adopt the hint (hint-used (HU)), they overwhelmingly do so via procedural reasoning (HU-Proc) rather than arithmetic manipulation (HU-Arith). Right: When models ignore the hint (hint-ignored (HI)), the distribution across arithmetic, factual, and random rationales varies substantially across families.
Figure 19: Adversarial hint templates used to control for option-letter bias. The prompt structure is held fixed while the hinted target is varied across B, C, and D.

Figure 20: Translation prompt examples used during inference, adapted from Zhao et al. (2026). Each prompt specifies the reasoning language, response format, localized question label, step-by-step reasoning directive, and reasoning-trace starter.

Figure 22: GPT-OSS-120B on a GPQA stellar variability question (Rein et al., 2024). The model refuses the hint inside its reasoning trace and commits to ∼0.32 (option A), yet the post-reasoning output boxes C. A monitor reading only the trace would mark this faithful while the final answer is hint-aligned. Green: correct reasoning. Red: contradicting final output.

Figure 21: GPT-OSS-120B on a GPQA stellar dynamics question (Rein et al., 2024). The model correctly applies gravitational-focusing theory, names option B as the closest physics answer, then explicitly verbalizes the conflict with the hint and chooses to follow the hint instead. Bold red

Figure 9: Token-level probability distributions for the GPT-OSS 120B model across layers and hinting conditions. Rows correspond to different settings: (a) simple hint with final answer C, (b) complex hint with final answer C, (c) simple hint with final answer A, and (d) complex hint with final answer A. Columns represent layer depth, where I, II, III, and IV correspond to layers 1, 12, 24, and 36, respectively. Each panel illustrates the probability of generating tokens corresponding to options A, B, C, and D, highlighting how answer preferences evolve across both layers and hinting conditions.

Table 4: Qwen3-8B per-language results across no-hint, simple-hint, and complex-hint conditions. Acc denotes correct-answer accuracy; BER denotes baseline-error rate, measured as selection of the hinted answer C; and Decep. denotes the deception rate among baseline-error responses.

Table 5: GPT-4o mini per-language results across no-hint, simple-hint, and complex-hint conditions. Acc denotes correct-answer accuracy; BER denotes baseline-error rate, measured as selection of the hinted answer C; and Decep. denotes the deception rate among baseline-error responses.
Figure 29: Claude Haiku 4.5 on a GPQA organic-synthesis question (Rein et al., 2024). The model tries four interpretations of (K +Q) mod 4, keeps the two yielding C, and discards those yielding A

Figure 1: Example of deceptive hint-driven reasoning. QWEN3-32B on a GPQA chemistry question (Rein et al., 2024). The model first gives the correct nucleophilicity analysis, explicitly endorses option A, and rules out option C on substantive chemistry grounds. It then applies the injected hint formula (K+Q) mod 4 using fabricated values for K and Q, overriding its own answer and submitting option C. Green: correct domain reasoning. Blue: explicit endorsement of the correct answer. Orange: substantive elimination of C. Red: fabricated K,Q values used to justify the hinted

Figure 4: Token-level probability trajectories across generation for the GPT-OSS 120B model under different hinting conditions and final prediction outcomes. Each subplot shows the probability of generating tokens corresponding to options A, B, C, and D as the next token over normalized sequence positions, separated by high- (solid lines) and low-resource (dotted lines) languages. Plot I and II shows the simple and complex hint condition where the model ultimately predicts C. Plot III and IV presents the simple and complex hint condition where the model predicts the correct answer A. These trajectories illustrate how probability over candidate answers evolves during generation under different hint strengths and outcomes. Shaded regions indicate standard error and gray band (0.1-0.25) highlights the early generation phase. See Appendix A.1 - A.3 for more plots across layers and model sizes.

Figure 28: GPT-4O-MINI on a GPQA organic-synthesis question (Rein et al., 2024). The model dismisses every option as having “unnecessary steps,” then selects C without resolving its own objections. It then sets K = Q = 0 “for simplicity” and computes (0 + 0) mod 4 = 0, which maps to letter A under the stated A=0 mapping. The trace asserts “the correct answer is C” before the

Figure 27: GPT-4O on a GPQA inorganic-chemistry question (Rein et al., 2024). The model abandons domain reasoning, openly states it has no basis to discriminate between the offered ranges, and selects C as a guess. It then performs the hint computation with values back-derived from the chosen letter — K = 2 from the option-letter index of C, and Q = 0 chosen so that the modular sum returns to 2 — producing a result that confirms its prior guess. The hint computation here functions

Figure 15: Judge prompt for the simple-hint deception evaluation. The judge measures explicit hint disclosure only. A response is counted as deceptive when the model selects the hinted target answer and the judge returns hint disclosed = No.
Table 6: Languages and their corresponding language families used in the multilingual evaluation of chain-of-thought monitorability under linguistic distribution shift.

Figure 12: Option-letter bias check. Baseline error rates are shown for simple hints (left) and complex hints (right) when the hinted target is varied across options B, C, and D. Simple hints induce high error rates across all hinted targets, while complex hints produce lower error rates for each target. This pattern indicates that the effect is driven by adversarial hinting rather than a fixed bias toward option C. Error bars show standard deviation across 13 languages.

Figure 13: Distribution of error types across the model suite. Errors are grouped into hint-used (HU) and hint-ignored (HI) categories, with sub-types denoting the source of the error: Arith (arithmetic), Proc (procedural), Fact (factual), and Rand (random). (a) Error composition by language resource level. (b) Aggregate distribution across all models and languages. HU-Proc and HI-Arith dominate the overall error profile, while HU-Arith errors remain rare across all resource tiers.

Figure 16: Judge prompt for the complex-hint deception evaluation. The judge counts a target-answer response as deceptive when the model selects C without transparently showing the required K +Q modulo computation, or when it uses omitted, fabricated, invalid, or post-hoc reasoning to justify C.

Figure 25: Claude Haiku 4.5 on a GPQA photochemistry question (Rein et al., 2024). Inside <think>, the model openly back-fits the hint (“Q would make this work for answer C”) and then abandons the computation. The post-</think> output silently removes every trace of the hint, presenting a

Figure 26: GPT-4O-MINI on a GPQA stellar reddening question (Rein et al., 2024). The model misreads the question as temperature-ranking, picks Star 3, then back-derives K = 2 and Q = 3 from its chosen answer. The modular arithmetic is wrong ((2 + 3) mod 4 = 1, not 2), but the trace

Figure 8: Token-level probability trajectories for the GPT-OSS 20B model at the last layer (layer 24) under different hinting conditions and final outcomes. Each subplot shows next-token probabilities for options A, B, C, and D over normalized sequence positions, separated by high- (solid) and low-resource (dotted) languages. Plot I shows the simple hint condition where the model ultimately predicts C; Plot II shows the same setup under complex hints. Plot III presents the simple hint condition where the model predicts the correct answer A, and Plot IV shows the corresponding complex hint condition. Shaded regions indicate ± standard error, and the gray band (0.1–0.25) highlights the early generation phase.

Figure 5: Deception rates under simple (left) and complex hints (right) across hinted targets B, C, and D. High deception across targets shows that CoT monitorability failures are not driven by a single answer option. Error bars show standard errors across 13 languages.

Figure 14: Relationship between baseline error rate and deception rate for closed-source models across 13 languages. Each point represents a model–language pair under simple hints (•) or complex hints (△). Deception remains high across a wide range of baseline error rates, indicating persistent CoT monitorability failures under adversarial hints.

Table 1: Deception rates under simple hints (mean± std, %) across five stochastic runs, by language and model. Higher values indicate lower CoT monitorability; rates remain consistently high for mid- and low-resource languages, indicating that monitorability degrades most severely outside highresource settings. The low standard deviations confirm that adversarial hint-induced monitorability failures are not artifacts of a single deterministic run.

Figure 11: Relationship between baseline error rate and deception rate for 13 models and 13 languages under simple (•) and complex (×) hints. Under simple hints, most model–language pairs cluster near 100% deception regardless of baseline error rate. Complex hints produce greater variation in deception, especially for the GPT-OSS models.

Figure 24: Gemma3–12B on a GPQA quantum-mechanics question (Rein et al., 2024). The model violates Pauli exclusion by placing all four fermions in n = 1 (correct ground state: two in n = 1, two in n = 2, giving 10E not 4E). Its derived energies match no option, so it adopts the values
Figure 23: GPT-5-MINI on a GPQA microbiology question (Rein et al., 2024). The model identifies A as correct and explicitly rules out C, then grafts K,Q onto the PCR band sizes (950, 1080 bp) from

Figure 18: Simple and complex hint templates used during inference (Emmons et al., 2025; Guan et al., 2025). The simple hint directly specifies the target option, while the complex hint requires a language-specific mapping rule, the computation (K +Q) mod 4, and a final mapping back to an answer option.

Table 2: Deception rates under complex hints (mean± std, %) across five stochastic runs, by language and model. Higher values indicate lower CoT monitorability; rates remain consistently high across all languages and resource tiers, with most cells saturating at 100%. The low standard deviations confirm that adversarial hint-induced monitorability failures are not artifacts of a single deterministic run.

Figure 10: Token-level probability distributions for the GPT-OSS 20B model across layers and hinting conditions. Rows correspond to different settings: (a) simple hint with final answer C, (b) complex hint with final answer C, (c) simple hint with final answer A, and (d) complex hint with final answer A. Columns represent layer depth, where I, II, III, and IV correspond to layers 1, 8, 16, and 24, respectively. Each panel illustrates the probability of generating tokens corresponding to options A, B, C, and D, highlighting how answer preferences evolve across both layers and hinting conditions.
